Title: Module 4 Basic Computer Skills: Understanding File Types in Windows
Summary: This resource explains how file types work in Windows, covering the relationship between file formats and extensions, how to show and change extensions, and common file types encountered in programming contexts. It also provides practical guidance on creating CSV files using Notepad and working with Google Drive and Google Colab for file access.
Author: Course Instructor (Internal Course Material)
RelevanceToModule: Understanding file types and extensions is foundational for Module 4 topics such as reading and writing text and CSV files, as students must be able to locate, create, and manage files correctly before working with Python's file I/O and the with-statement context manager. The section on Google Drive and Colab directly supports workflows where students may load or save files in cloud environments while practicing file handling and data processing scripts.
PedagogicalPurpose: To assist users in basic skills that might be lacking.

Module Description: Covers writing and importing custom modules, using standard library modules, reading and writing text and CSV files with the with-statement context manager, working with JSON, and applying try-except patterns for graceful error handling in engineering scripts.

} } Module 4 Basic Computer Skills Understanding File Types in Windows How your computer knows what a file is, how to change that, what breaks when you do, and how to hand-build a CSV using nothing but Notepad or skip Windows entirely and host it on Google Drive for Colab. In this module What a file type is How extensions identify files Showing file extensions Changing a file type Side effects of changing Common file types Make a CSV in Notepad Google Drive + Colab 01 What a file type actually is Every file on a computer is just a long sequence of bytes. On its own, that sequence means nothing it could be a photo, a spreadsheet, or a program. A file type is the agreement about how those bytes should be interpreted. Windows uses that agreement to decide which icon to show, which program to open the file with, and how to treat the contents. There are really two layers to a file type. The format is the internal structure of the bytes (for example, the rules that make a JPEG a JPEG). The extension is the short label at the end of the filename that advertises which format Windows should expect. Most of the time these two agree but as you'll see, they don't have to, and that's where things get interesting. 02 How extensions identify files In Windows, the part of a filename after the last dot is the exten
